reasoning: The sink is confirmed at templates/bsr-help.php line 62: '<textarea ...><?php echo BSR_Compatibility::get_sysinfo(); ?></textarea>' with no esc_textarea() wrapping. The missing escaping is a genuine WordPress coding standards violation. However, the XSS/HTML-injection threat is severely limited by the input sources: get_sysinfo() aggregates $_SERVER['SERVER_SOFTWARE'] (set by the web server, not by HTTP request headers in Apache/Nginx), plugin Name/Version headers (read from plugin files on disk), and server-side PHP/WP constants — none of which are controllable by a lower-privilege web request. Injecting '</textarea>' into plugin Name requires installing a malicious plugin, which itself requires administrator access. The reachability claim (admin-only) is correct; the page is only accessible to users who can reach the BSR admin panel. There is no privilege-escalation vector: the only users who could craft a payload are already administrators. The finding is real as a coding-standards miss (esc_textarea() is absent, the pattern is verified in the code), but the practical exploitability is negligible — it is a self-XSS scenario at best.
